option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows consistent gas yield difference but does not isolate geometry as the sole variable; other factors (chemistry, electrode design) are uncontrolled.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: LFP-SS has higher gas volume yet lower temperature, directly falsifying the claim that gas volume primarily determines peak temperature.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: LFP-SS lower gas/Ah yet lower peak temperature inverts the intuitive safety ranking, making the design inference inadmissible without further evidence.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of inferring thermal hazard rank from gas yield rank across battery formats
analysis.primary_boundary: Gas production per Ah and peak temperature are not monotonically coupled; LFP-SS higher gas/Ah yet lower temperature breaks the single-variable inference
intended_error_modes: causal overclaim from gas yield to thermal energy
single-variable geometry attribution
mechanism substitution (gas volume drives temperature)
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring total energy release from gas volume alone
Attributing format geometry as sole gas yield driver
Generalizing temperature rank from gas rank

solver_view.stem: An LFP-SS cell undergoes thermal runaway at 100% SOC. Tests in air yield a peak pressure change of 0.82 kPa/Ah, while identical tests in nitrogen yield 0.62 kPa/Ah. A safety engineer proposes that conducting thermal runaway tests exclusively in nitrogen provides a conservative (worst-case) pressure estimate for facility design. A second engineer argues that the flammability envelope of the vented gas must also be evaluated. At 110% SOC, the upper explosive limit (UEL) of LFP-SS gas is lower than that of LFP-L, despite LFP-SS producing more gas per Ah. Which statements are admissible?
options: A. The nitrogen environment produces a lower peak pressure change than air, so using nitrogen test data alone underestimates the pressure load relevant to air-environment facility design.
B. Because LFP-SS produces more gas per Ah than LFP-L at 110% SOC, its vented gas mixture necessarily presents a wider flammability range than LFP-L under the same conditions.

C. The reduction in UEL for LFP-SS at 110% SOC relative to 100% SOC is consistent with a shift in hydrocarbon gas composition toward lower-carbon species that individually have narrower upper flammability limits.
D. The higher pressure change observed in air compared to nitrogen can be attributed to oxidative reactions involving ejected material interacting with ambient oxygen after venting.
E. Nitrogen environment testing is sufficient for all safety assessments because it captures the maximum chemical energy release from internal decomposition reactions.
F. A facility designed using only nitrogen-environment pressure data may be inadequately rated if the operational environment contains oxygen.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Air yields 0.82 kPa/Ah vs. 0.62 kPa/Ah in N2; nitrogen data underestimates air-environment pressure, making it non-conservative for facility design.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: At 110% SOC, LFP-SS UEL is lower than LFP-L despite higher gas/Ah, showing gas volume does not determine flammability range width.
option_judgments.error_type: causal overclaim from gas volume to flammability range

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states CxHy decomposes further into CH4 and C2H6 at higher SOC, reducing CxHy content; lower-carbon species shift UEL downward.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly attributes the air–N2 pressure difference to oxygen exacerbating combustion and decomposition after gas ejection.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Nitrogen suppresses post-ejection oxidative reactions; air data shows higher pressure, meaning nitrogen does not capture maximum energy release in oxygen-present environments.
option_judgments.error_type: conservative but incorrect — ignores post-ejection oxidative pathway

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Air environment produces 32% higher pressure change per Ah; nitrogen-only design basis is non-conservative for air-exposed facilities.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of nitrogen-environment test data as conservative design basis for pressure and flammability in air-exposed facilities
analysis.primary_boundary: Post-ejection oxidative reactions in air elevate pressure beyond nitrogen baseline; nitrogen data is not conservative for air environments
intended_error_modes: treating inert-atmosphere test as worst-case
inferring flammability range width from gas volume
ignoring post-ejection reaction pathway
disallowed_extrapolations: Assuming nitrogen captures maximum energy release
Inferring UEL from gas quantity rather than composition

solver_view.stem: At 100% SOC, LFP-SS gas has both a higher lower explosive limit (LEL) and a higher upper explosive limit (UEL) than LFP-L gas. At 110% SOC, the UEL relationship reverses: LFP-SS UEL becomes lower than LFP-L UEL. The reversal is linked to changes in the hydrocarbon fraction of the vented gas. A process safety engineer must characterize ignition hazard for both cell types across SOC levels. Which of the following conclusions are admissible?
options: A. At 100% SOC, LFP-SS gas requires a higher minimum concentration to ignite than LFP-L gas, meaning LFP-SS presents a lower ignition risk at lean mixture conditions near the LEL.

B. The SOC-dependent reversal of the UEL relationship means that ranking the two cell types by ignition hazard at one SOC level cannot be reliably extrapolated to the other SOC level without composition data.
C. Because LFP-SS has a higher LEL at 100% SOC, its vented gas is safer across all mixture concentrations and at all SOC levels compared to LFP-L.
D. The decrease in higher-carbon hydrocarbon content at elevated SOC in LFP-SS is consistent with more intense internal reactions converting complex hydrocarbons into simpler species.
E. At 110% SOC, LFP-L gas poses a greater ignition hazard at rich mixture conditions near the UEL than LFP-SS gas, because LFP-L's UEL is higher under those conditions.
F. The flammability window width (UEL minus LEL) is necessarily larger for LFP-SS than LFP-L at both SOC levels because LFP-SS produces more total gas volume per Ah.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Higher LEL for LFP-SS at 100% SOC means a greater concentration is needed to reach ignition threshold, reducing lean-mixture ignition risk.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: UEL ranking reverses between 100% and 110% SOC; a single-SOC ranking cannot be generalized without composition information at each SOC.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Higher LEL applies only at 100% SOC and only near lean conditions; the UEL reversal at 110% SOC and rich-mixture behavior are not captured by this claim.
option_judgments.error_type: overgeneralization across SOC and mixture concentration range

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states more intense reactions at 110% SOC decompose CxHy into CH4 and C2H6, consistent with conversion of complex to simpler hydrocarbon species.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: At 110% SOC, LFP-L UEL exceeds LFP-SS UEL; a higher UEL extends the rich-mixture ignition range, increasing hazard near the upper limit.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Flammability window depends on gas composition, not gas volume; the UEL reversal at 110% SOC directly contradicts a volume-based flammability width ranking.
option_judgments.error_type: mechanism substitution — gas volume substituted for composition as flammability determinant

analysis.target_cognitive_object: SOC-dependent admissibility of ignition hazard rankings based on LEL/UEL reversal and hydrocarbon composition shift
analysis.primary_boundary: Flammability limits are composition-dependent, not volume-dependent; rankings are regime-specific and cannot be extrapolated across SOC levels
intended_error_modes: overgeneralizing LEL advantage to all conditions
inferring flammability window from gas volume
ignoring SOC-dependent composition shift
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying 100% SOC LEL/UEL ranking to 110% SOC
Inferring flammability range from gas production rate alone

solver_view.prompt: Two lithium iron phosphate battery formats are tested under thermal runaway conditions. The small-format cell (LFP-SS) consistently produces more gas per unit capacity than the large-format cell (LFP-L) across all test conditions, yet LFP-L reaches higher peak temperatures. Additionally, when SOC is raised from 100% to 110%, the ratio of LFP-L peak temperature to LFP-SS peak temperature increases. In an air environment, LFP-SS shows higher peak pressure change per Ah than in nitrogen, with the difference attributed to reactions occurring after gas is ejected from the cell. A process safety engineer must assess whether gas production per Ah is a reliable proxy for thermal severity and whether inert-atmosphere pressure data is conservative for air-environment facility design. Provide a mechanistically justified assessment of both questions, identifying the specific reasoning that supports or refutes each proxy relationship.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Gas production per Ah is not a reliable proxy for thermal severity. LFP-SS consistently produces more gas per Ah than LFP-L yet achieves lower peak temperatures under all tested conditions. This decoupling indicates that gas yield and peak temperature are governed by at least partially independent mechanisms—gas volume reflects the extent of decomposition reactions producing gaseous species, while peak temperature reflects the total heat released by all exothermic reactions, including those that do not produce gas. The widening temperature ratio at 110% SOC further shows that the thermal response of LFP-L amplifies more strongly with increasing energy content than LFP-SS, reinforcing that format-specific internal reaction pathways—not gas yield—control thermal severity. Regarding inert-atmosphere pressure data: nitrogen-environment testing produces lower peak pressure change per Ah than air-environment testing for LFP-SS at 100% SOC (0.62 vs. 0.82 kPa/Ah). The higher air-environment value is mechanistically attributed to oxygen in the surrounding atmosphere reacting with ejected gas and material after venting, exacerbating combustion and decomposition. Because this post-ejection oxidative pathway is absent in nitrogen, nitrogen-environment pressure data underestimates the pressure load in air-exposed facilities. Therefore, nitrogen-environment data is not conservative for facility pressure design in air environments; it represents a lower bound, not an upper bound, on pressure hazard.
must_include: Gas production per Ah is not a reliable proxy for thermal severity because LFP-SS higher gas/Ah corresponds to lower peak temperature than LFP-L
Gas yield and peak temperature reflect different underlying reaction pathways
Nitrogen environment yields lower pressure change per Ah than air environment
The air–nitrogen pressure difference is caused by post-ejection oxidative reactions with ambient oxygen
Nitrogen-environment pressure data underestimates air-environment pressure and is therefore not conservative for air-exposed facility design
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming gas volume is the primary driver of peak temperature
Asserting nitrogen testing is conservative or worst-case for all safety parameters
Attributing the temperature difference solely to cell geometry without acknowledging reaction pathway differences
Generalizing the air–nitrogen pressure ratio to LFP-L or to other SOC levels without evidence

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of gas production per Ah as a thermal severity proxy and of nitrogen-environment pressure data as a conservative design basis
analysis.primary_boundary: Both proxy relationships fail: gas yield and thermal severity are decoupled; nitrogen pressure is a lower bound, not an upper bound, in air environments
intended_error_modes: Treating higher gas production as implying higher thermal hazard
Assuming inert-atmosphere testing captures worst-case pressure
Ignoring post-ejection oxidative reaction pathway in air
Conflating format geometry with reaction pathway as the sole explanatory variable

solver_view.stem: A 314 Ah LiFePO4 battery undergoes thermal runaway under overheating conditions, releasing a gas mixture dominated by H2 (~53%), with CO2 (~22%) and CO (~14%) as secondary components. Explosion testing reveals that the maximum overpressure occurs at a gas-air concentration of approximately 15.86%, substantially below the theoretically expected stoichiometric concentration of 28.19%. Which of the following conclusions about this system are supported by engineering reasoning from these conditions?
options: A. The shift of peak overpressure concentration below the stoichiometric value is consistent with CO2 and CO within the mixture interfering with the chain-branching radical reactions that sustain combustion.

B. Because H2 is the dominant species, the mixture behaves as a pure H2-air system for explosion overpressure prediction purposes, and deviations from stoichiometric peak are attributable solely to H2 dilution.
C. The presence of CO2 and CO as suppressants implies that the mixture cannot reach its theoretical maximum overpressure even when ignited under controlled conditions.
D. The lower-than-stoichiometric peak concentration indicates that the mixture's flammability limits are narrower than those of pure H2, making ignition less probable across all concentration ranges.

E. Predicting explosion overpressure using a single-component model for this gas mixture would overestimate the actual peak pressure, because the multi-component composition introduces suppressive interactions not captured by single-fuel kinetics.
F. The deviation between actual and stoichiometric peak concentration proves that CO2 and CO are present in quantities sufficient to completely prevent explosion under any ignition condition.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly attributes the below-stoichiometric peak to CO2 and CO suppressing chain reaction mechanisms underlying gas explosions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Treating the mixture as pure H2 ignores the documented suppressive role of CO2 and CO, which is the primary cause of the deviation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows the reaction fails to complete even under prescribed conditions due to CO2 and CO suppression, meaning theoretical maximum is not reached.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence addresses peak overpressure concentration, not flammability limits; inferring narrower limits from peak shift is an unsupported scope expansion.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Multi-component suppressive interactions cause incomplete reaction; single-component kinetics cannot capture this, so single-fuel models would overestimate peak pressure.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows suppression shifts peak concentration and limits reaction completeness, but the mixture remains within flammability limits and explodes; complete prevention is not supported.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanism by which multi-component TR gas composition shifts peak explosion overpressure below stoichiometric concentration
analysis.primary_boundary: Suppression shifts peak and limits reaction completeness but does not prevent explosion entirely; single-component models are inadequate
intended_error_modes: mechanism_substitution treating mixture as pure H2
scope_expansion to flammability limits
causal_overclaim that suppression prevents all explosion
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring narrower flammability limits from peak shift
Claiming complete explosion prevention from partial suppression

solver_view.stem: After an initial explosion of overheated LiFePO4 thermal runaway gas at a mixture concentration of 22.21% (lean oxygen state), residual gas analysis shows CO content increased by approximately 281%, H2 content decreased by approximately 49%, and CH4 content decreased by approximately 80% relative to pre-explosion values. The pre-explosion lower explosion limit of the mixture was 6.32%, and the post-explosion residual gas lower explosion limit is determined to be 12.95%. A safety engineer must assess secondary explosion risk and gas consumption ordering. Which statements are admissible?
options: A. The post-explosion residual gas retains flammability, because its lower explosion limit of 12.95% is still a finite positive value indicating an ignitable concentration range exists.

B. Under lean oxygen conditions, CH4 is consumed more completely than H2 during the explosion, consistent with CH4 having a higher reactivity ranking than H2 in this mixture under oxygen-limited conditions.

C. The large post-explosion increase in CO concentration is consistent with incomplete oxidation of carbon-containing species under oxygen-limited conditions, where CO is produced but not further oxidized to CO2.
D. Because the post-explosion LEL of 12.95% exceeds the pre-explosion LEL of 6.32%, the residual gas mixture poses no secondary explosion hazard at concentrations below 12.95%.

E. The consumption ordering of gases during explosion, with heavier hydrocarbons consumed preferentially over lighter species, is consistent with a negative correlation between consumption rate and the Mulliken charge on hydrogen atoms in each species.
F. The shift in gas composition after explosion at 22.21% proves that the mixture has fully transitioned outside all flammability limits and secondary ignition is physically impossible.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: A finite positive LEL of 12.95% confirms the residual gas remains flammable within a defined concentration range; secondary explosion risk persists.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows CH4 is consumed more than H2 (80% vs 49%), but the consumption ordering correlates with Mulliken charge, not a simple reactivity ranking under oxygen-limited conditions as stated.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Lean oxygen state prevents full oxidation of CO to CO2; CO accumulation is mechanistically consistent with incomplete combustion under oxygen-limited conditions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: A higher LEL means ignition requires higher concentration, but hazard below 12.95% is not eliminated; it is reduced. The statement misinterprets LEL as a safety threshold eliminating all risk.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states consumption order negatively correlates with Mulliken charge on hydrogen atoms, with heavier hydrocarbons consumed preferentially.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states secondary explosion risk must still be mitigated after initial explosion; the residual gas retains a finite LEL confirming continued flammability.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Post-explosion residual gas composition, secondary explosion risk, and gas consumption ordering under lean oxygen conditions
analysis.primary_boundary: Elevated post-explosion LEL reduces but does not eliminate secondary explosion risk; consumption ordering is governed by Mulliken charge correlation, not generic reactivity
intended_error_modes: misinterpreting higher LEL as eliminating hazard
claiming complete flammability elimination
misattributing consumption ordering to simple reactivity ranking
disallowed_extrapolations: Concluding secondary explosion is impossible from elevated LEL
Generalizing consumption ordering to all oxygen conditions

solver_view.stem: A safety engineer compares thermal runaway gas generation from 314 Ah LiFePO4 cells under two abuse modes: overheating and overcharging. Overcharging produces approximately 240 L of gas versus approximately 190 L under overheating. Gas composition proportions are similar across both modes, with H2 dominant in both cases. The engineer must assess explosion hazard differences between the two abuse scenarios and determine which conclusions about comparative risk are defensible.
options: A. Because overcharging generates approximately 26% more gas volume than overheating, the absolute quantity of flammable H2 released under overcharging exceeds that under overheating, even though H2 constitutes a slightly lower proportion of the overcharging gas.

B. The similar compositional proportions of CO2 and CO across both abuse modes imply that the degree of chain-reaction suppression during explosion would be approximately equivalent for gases from either mode at the same mixture concentration.

C. Because overcharging produces more total gas, the explosion overpressure generated from overcharging gas at its stoichiometric concentration will necessarily exceed that generated from overheating gas at the same concentration.

D. The higher gas volume under overcharging means that in a confined space, overcharging presents a greater risk of reaching a flammable concentration than overheating, assuming equivalent ventilation conditions.
E. Since H2 proportion is slightly lower under overcharging (47.64%) than overheating (53.12%), overcharging gas is less hazardous than overheating gas on a per-liter-released basis.

F. The difference in total gas volume between the two abuse modes is sufficient to conclude that the electrochemical decomposition pathways activated during overcharging are more extensive than those activated during overheating.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 240.56 L × 47.64% = ~114.6 L H2 under overcharging vs 190.18 L × 53.12% = ~101 L H2 under overheating; absolute H2 volume is higher under overcharging despite lower proportion.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: CO2 proportions are 22.07% vs 23.86% and CO proportions are 13.75% vs 16.86%; similar enough that suppressive effects on chain reactions would be approximately comparable at equal mixture concentrations.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Explosion overpressure at stoichiometric concentration depends on gas composition and reaction kinetics, not total volume produced; more gas volume does not imply higher peak overpressure per unit volume.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Greater total gas volume released in a confined space with equivalent ventilation increases the probability of reaching flammable concentrations; this is a direct consequence of higher gas generation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Lower H2 proportion does not make overcharging gas less hazardous per liter; absolute H2 per liter is similar, and CO proportion is higher under overcharging, maintaining comparable or greater hazard.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Gas volume difference reflects overall generation magnitude but is insufficient evidence to conclude which specific electrochemical decomposition pathways are activated or their relative extent.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Comparative explosion hazard between overcharging and overheating TR gas, accounting for both volume and composition differences
analysis.primary_boundary: Higher total volume increases absolute flammable gas release and confinement risk, but does not determine per-volume explosion overpressure; compositional similarity bounds suppression equivalence
intended_error_modes: confusing proportion with absolute quantity
inferring peak overpressure from total volume
inferring decomposition pathway extent from volume alone
disallowed_extrapolations: Concluding overcharging is less hazardous from lower H2 proportion
Inferring electrochemical pathway details from gas volume alone

solver_view.prompt: During an explosion of thermal runaway gas from an overheated LiFePO4 battery at a mixture concentration of 22.21% in air, the system is in a lean oxygen state. Post-explosion analysis shows CO content rises sharply while H2 and CH4 are substantially consumed. The post-explosion residual gas has a lower explosion limit of 12.95%, compared to the pre-explosion lower explosion limit of 6.32%. A process safety engineer argues that because the post-explosion LEL has nearly doubled, the secondary explosion hazard has been effectively eliminated and no further mitigation is needed. Evaluate this argument. In your answer, address: (1) what the elevated post-explosion LEL physically means for secondary explosion risk, (2) why the change in gas composition after the initial explosion does not eliminate the hazard, and (3) what the post-explosion CO accumulation implies for the chemical state of the residual gas.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The engineer's argument is not defensible. (1) An elevated post-explosion LEL of 12.95% means that a higher concentration of residual gas is required to form an ignitable mixture compared to the pre-explosion state, but a finite positive LEL still defines a flammable range. The hazard is reduced in the sense that lower concentrations are no longer ignitable, but any accumulation above 12.95% remains capable of supporting ignition and secondary explosion. The LEL doubling does not eliminate the flammable range; it shifts its lower boundary upward. (2) The post-explosion gas composition still contains H2, CO, and residual hydrocarbons in quantities sufficient to sustain flammability above the new LEL. The evidence explicitly states that secondary explosion risk must still be mitigated after the initial event. The change in composition alters the mixture's flammability characteristics but does not remove all flammable species. (3) The large post-explosion increase in CO content is mechanistically consistent with incomplete oxidation under lean oxygen conditions: carbon-containing species such as CH4 are partially oxidized to CO rather than fully to CO2, because insufficient oxygen is available to complete the reaction. This means the residual gas contains a higher proportion of CO, which is itself a flammable and toxic species, indicating that the chemical energy content of the mixture has been partially redistributed rather than fully released. The residual gas therefore remains both flammable and chemically reactive.
must_include: Elevated LEL reduces but does not eliminate the flammable concentration range
Secondary explosion risk explicitly remains after initial explosion
CO increase is consistent with incomplete oxidation under lean oxygen conditions
Residual gas retains flammable species above the new LEL
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming the doubled LEL proves secondary explosion is impossible
Inferring that CO accumulation indicates the mixture is inert
Concluding that post-explosion gas composition is safe without specifying concentration conditions

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Interpretation of post-explosion LEL elevation and residual gas composition in terms of secondary explosion hazard and incomplete combustion mechanism
analysis.primary_boundary: Elevated LEL shifts but does not eliminate flammability; CO accumulation reflects incomplete oxidation, not inertness
intended_error_modes: Misinterpreting higher LEL as hazard elimination
Ignoring that residual gas retains flammable species
Misattributing CO increase to inert dilution rather than incomplete oxidation

solver_view.stem: A 280Ah LiFePO4 prismatic cell undergoes thermal runaway testing across multiple SOC levels. Engineers observe that maximum cell temperature increases disproportionately above 50% SOC, with gas production at 75% SOC reaching 7.4 times the 25% SOC baseline. The vent gas composition shifts substantially: CO2 fraction drops markedly while H2 fraction rises with increasing SOC. Given these conditions, which of the following conclusions about the 75% SOC vent gas are admissible?
options: A. The lower flammability limit of the 75% SOC vent gas is lower than that of the 25% SOC vent gas, indicating the 75% SOC mixture ignites more readily.
B. Because gas volume at 75% SOC is the largest among the tested conditions, the flammable range of the 75% SOC vent gas is also the widest.

C. The upper flammability limit of the 75% SOC vent gas is substantially narrower than that of the 50% SOC vent gas, meaning the 75% SOC gas mixture has a compressed flammable range despite greater thermal severity.
D. The elevated H2 fraction at 75% SOC is the sole driver of increased ignition risk, and CO2 fraction changes are mechanistically irrelevant to flammability.
E. The flammable range of the 75% SOC vent gas is lower than that of both the 25% and 50% SOC vent gases, which is counterintuitive given the higher gas production volume.
F. The deflagration risk of the gas mixture is highest at 75% SOC because thermal severity and gas volume both peak at that condition.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: LFL at 75% SOC (~5%) is lower than at 25% SOC (10–15%), directly supporting easier ignition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Gas volume does not determine flammable range; RF at 75% SOC (17.6%) is the narrowest, not widest.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: UFL at 75% SOC (4.7–6.6%) is far below 50% SOC (55–65%), compressing the flammable range despite higher thermal severity.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: CO2 reduction alters electrolyte decomposition pathways and affects flammability limits; attributing risk solely to H2 is an unsupported single-variable claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: RF at 75% SOC (17.6%) is lower than at 25% (43.7%) and 50% (48.5%), counterintuitive given the largest gas volume.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Deflagration risk peaks at 50% SOC, not 75%, despite 75% SOC having greater thermal severity and gas volume.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Non-monotonic relationship between SOC, vent gas composition, and flammability parameters including LFL, UFL, and flammable range
analysis.primary_boundary: Thermal severity and gas volume do not monotonically determine flammable range or deflagration risk; composition-driven flammability limits must be evaluated independently
intended_error_modes: unjustified monotonicity from gas volume to flammable range
single-variable attribution of flammability to H2 alone
misidentifying 75% SOC as peak deflagration risk
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring flammable range rank directly from gas production volume
Treating H2 increase as the only flammability-relevant compositional change

solver_view.stem: During thermal runaway testing of 280Ah LiFePO4 prismatic cells, cells at 0%, 25%, and 50% SOC all show a temperature rise followed by a subsequent decrease after prolonged heating, with peak temperatures of 77°C, 92.3°C, and 251.1°C respectively. Cells above 50% SOC exhibit full thermal runaway. The Flammability Hazard Index ranks as 50% SOC > 75% SOC > 25% SOC. An engineer must assess safety implications for a storage system operating between 30% and 60% SOC. Which conclusions are admissible?
options: A. Restricting operating SOC to below 50% eliminates thermal runaway risk for this cell chemistry under the tested heating conditions.
B. A cell stored at 50% SOC that does not progress to full thermal runaway under prolonged heating may still generate vent gas with a higher flammability hazard index than a cell at 75% SOC.
C. Because the 50% SOC cell reached 251.1°C without full thermal runaway, its vent gas flammability risk is necessarily lower than that of a 75% SOC cell that undergoes complete thermal runaway.
D. The non-monotonic behavior of the Flammability Hazard Index with SOC means that reducing SOC from 75% to 50% does not reduce the flammability hazard of emitted gases.
E. The critical threshold behavior at 50% SOC implies that cells operating near this boundary require flammability hazard assessment independent of thermal runaway occurrence.
F. Since cells below 50% SOC do not exhibit full thermal runaway, gas emission volumes at those SOC levels are negligible and pose no meaningful flammability concern.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows 50% SOC itself is the critical threshold; operating below it reduces but the evidence does not support 'eliminates' TR risk under all conditions.
option_judgments.error_type: protective mechanism overclaim / absolute-language boundary violation

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: FHI at 50% SOC exceeds that at 75% SOC; a 50% SOC cell that vents without full TR can still produce gas with higher FHI than a 75% SOC cell.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: This inverts the FHI ordering; 50% SOC vent gas has higher FHI than 75% SOC, so lower peak temperature does not imply lower flammability hazard.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: FHI is higher at 50% than 75% SOC; reducing from 75% to 50% increases flammability hazard, confirming non-monotonic behavior.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The threshold at 50% SOC and non-monotonic FHI behavior require flammability assessment even when full TR does not occur.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Cells at 25% SOC still produce vent gas with LFL of 10–15% and RF ~43.7%; absence of full TR does not imply negligible flammability concern.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Decoupling of thermal runaway occurrence from flammability hazard ranking; non-monotonic FHI behavior and its safety implications
analysis.primary_boundary: Absence of full thermal runaway does not imply low flammability hazard; FHI is non-monotonic with SOC and must be assessed independently
intended_error_modes: equating no-TR outcome with no flammability risk
assuming FHI increases monotonically with SOC
treating 50% SOC threshold as a safe lower bound
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming SOC restriction below 50% eliminates TR risk under all conditions
Inferring flammability hazard rank from thermal severity alone

solver_view.stem: Thermal runaway tests on 280Ah LiFePO4 prismatic cells show that CO2 molar fraction in vent gas drops from 61% at 25% SOC to 12% at 75% SOC, while H2 rises from 7.2% to 26.1%. The number of detected gaseous components increases from 15 to 36 across the same range. Gas production at 75% SOC is 7.4 times that at 25% SOC. An engineer attributes the narrowing of the flammable range at 75% SOC entirely to dilution by the larger total gas volume. Which assessment of this attribution is correct?
options: A. The attribution is mechanistically unsupported because flammable range is determined by mixture composition, not total gas volume; the shift in CO2 and H2 fractions directly alters the flammability limits.
B. The attribution is partially correct because larger gas volume does reduce the effective concentration of flammable species in an enclosed space.
C. The attribution is consistent with the evidence because gas production volume and flammable range both scale with SOC in the same direction.

D. The attribution is incorrect because the upper flammability limit at 75% SOC is far lower than at 50% SOC, a change that cannot be explained by volume dilution and must reflect compositional effects.
E. The attribution is admissible if the storage enclosure is sufficiently large that gas concentration remains below the lower flammability limit.
F. The attribution is incorrect because the increase in detected gaseous components at 75% SOC introduces species that individually lower the upper flammability limit of the mixture.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Flammable range is a composition-dependent property; the large CO2 decrease and H2 increase directly shift LFL and UFL independent of volume.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Volume dilution in an enclosure is a separate system-level effect; it does not explain the intrinsic flammable range of the gas mixture itself.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Gas production volume increases with SOC but flammable range at 75% SOC (17.6%) is lower than at 25% (43.7%) and 50% (48.5%), contradicting co-directional scaling.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: UFL collapses from 55–65% at 50% SOC to 4.7–6.6% at 75% SOC; this dramatic shift is compositionally driven and cannot result from volume dilution.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Enclosure size affects dispersion but does not validate an attribution about the intrinsic flammable range of the emitted gas mixture.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: While additional species are detected at 75% SOC, the evidence attributes the UFL change to compositional shifts in CO2 and H2, not specifically to individual new species lowering UFL.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanism responsible for narrowing flammable range at 75% SOC: composition-driven versus volume-driven attribution
analysis.primary_boundary: Flammable range is an intrinsic compositional property; volume dilution is a system-level dispersion effect that cannot explain changes in LFL/UFL of the gas mixture itself
intended_error_modes: confusing gas volume with gas composition as the driver of flammability limits
assuming monotonic co-variation of gas volume and flammable range
attributing UFL collapse to new species rather than CO2/H2 ratio shift
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring that enclosure volume controls intrinsic gas flammability limits
Attributing UFL change solely to newly detected minor species without compositional evidence

solver_view.prompt: A safety engineer is designing a ventilation and fire suppression protocol for a battery energy storage system using 280Ah LiFePO4 prismatic cells. The system operates across a range of SOC levels. The engineer proposes that the highest flammability hazard from vent gas emissions occurs at the highest operational SOC, and therefore the suppression system should be sized for the 75% SOC worst-case scenario. Using engineering reasoning about vent gas composition and flammability properties, evaluate whether this proposal is justified, and identify what the evidence-supported worst-case scenario should be for two distinct flammability metrics.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The proposal is not justified. While 75% SOC produces the largest gas volume and highest thermal severity, flammability hazard does not increase monotonically with SOC. The Flammability Hazard Index is highest at 50% SOC, not 75% SOC, making 50% SOC the worst case for overall flammability hazard. For ignition risk (lower flammability limit), 75% SOC vent gas has a lower LFL (~5%) than 25% or 50% SOC gas (10–15%), making 75% SOC the worst case for ease of ignition. However, for deflagration risk and flammable range width, 50% SOC is the worst case: its upper flammability limit (55–65%) far exceeds that of 75% SOC (4.7–6.6%), and its flammable range (~48.5%) is the widest. The suppression system should therefore be sized considering 50% SOC as the primary worst case for deflagration and overall flammability hazard, while also accounting for the lower ignition threshold at 75% SOC.
must_include: FHI is highest at 50% SOC, not 75% SOC
flammability hazard does not increase monotonically with SOC
75% SOC has the lowest LFL (~5%), indicating easiest ignition
50% SOC has the widest flammable range and highest deflagration risk
UFL at 75% SOC (4.7–6.6%) is far lower than at 50% SOC (55–65%)
the proposal to use 75% SOC as the sole worst case is not supported
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming 75% SOC is the worst case for all flammability metrics
Claiming flammable range increases monotonically with SOC or gas volume
Recommending a specific suppression system design parameter not derivable from the evidence
Asserting that 50% SOC cells cannot produce dangerous vent gas because they may not reach full thermal runaway

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Non-monotonic relationship between SOC and multiple distinct flammability metrics; correct identification of worst-case SOC conditions for different hazard types
analysis.primary_boundary: Thermal severity and gas volume do not determine flammability hazard ranking; FHI, LFL, UFL, and flammable range must each be evaluated independently against SOC
intended_error_modes: assuming highest SOC equals highest flammability hazard for all metrics
conflating gas production volume with flammable range
failing to distinguish ignition risk (LFL-driven) from deflagration risk (UFL/RF-driven)

solver_view.stem: An LFP battery pack installed in a confined utility room undergoes thermal runaway at 100% SOC in a nitrogen-purged environment. Post-event gas sampling confirms a dramatically shifted composition compared to a 25% SOC event. A safety engineer must assess explosion hazard and toxic exposure risk for first responders entering the space. Which conclusions about the 100% SOC gas mixture are admissible based on the composition shift alone?
options: A. The fire-suppression effectiveness of the vented gas is reduced at 100% SOC relative to 25% SOC because the proportion of the primary fire-retardant component decreases substantially.
B. The explosive hazard window is wider at 100% SOC than at 25% SOC because the upper explosion limit expands significantly while the lower explosion limit remains comparatively stable.
C. The higher H2 fraction at 100% SOC guarantees that any ignition will produce a detonation rather than a deflagration.
D. At 100% SOC, the gas mixture is less hazardous to first responders because the dominant combustible component, H2, disperses more rapidly than heavier gases.
E. The shift in gas composition at 100% SOC means the mixture is more likely to sustain combustion once ignited, but does not by itself confirm whether ignition will occur.
F. Because CO2 proportion drops from approximately 50% to approximately 16% as SOC increases from 25% to 100%, a CO2-based suppression strategy effective at low SOC may be insufficient at high SOC.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: CO2 drops from 50.4% to 16.3% as SOC rises; CO2 is identified as the primary fire-retardant component, so suppression capacity decreases.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: UEL expands from ~17% to ~55% with increasing SOC while LEL remains 5–11%, directly widening the explosive window at high SOC.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence supports wider explosive window and higher combustible fraction but does not establish detonation vs. deflagration distinction; this is an unsupported overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Dispersal rate of H2 is not addressed in the evidence; this introduces a mechanism not supported by the evidence bundle and ignores the increased combustible fraction risk.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Higher H2 and wider UEL support greater post-ignition hazard; ignition probability depends on ignition sources not characterized by composition alone—admissible conditional conclusion.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: CO2 fraction at 100% SOC (~16%) is roughly one-third of its 25% SOC value (~50%), directly implying CO2-based suppression designed for low-SOC conditions may be undersized at high SOC.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of safety conclusions from SOC-dependent gas composition shifts in LFP thermal runaway
analysis.primary_boundary: Composition shift supports combustion and suppression hazard inferences but does not support detonation mode claims or dispersal-based safety arguments
intended_error_modes: causal_overclaim from H2 fraction to detonation
mechanism substitution using dispersal argument
ignoring CO2 suppression role
disallowed_extrapolations: detonation vs deflagration distinction from composition alone
dispersal rate inference from molecular weight without stem support

solver_view.stem: In a gas pipeline maintenance facility, LFP battery thermal runaway gases vent into a space where residual methane is present. Testing shows explosion overpressure peaks near 4–6% methane and reaches approximately 0.62 MPa, while free radical concentration peaks at 8–9% methane. A safety designer proposes that suppressing radical chain reactions at the 8–9% methane range will be the most effective strategy for reducing structural damage from overpressure. Evaluate the following claims about this proposal and the underlying explosion dynamics.
options: A. The proposal is mechanistically flawed because peak overpressure and peak radical concentration occur at different methane concentrations, indicating that radical activity alone does not govern macroscopic pressure output.
B. Targeting radical suppression at 8–9% methane is appropriate because that concentration range produces the highest free radical density, which directly causes peak overpressure.
C. At methane concentrations above 8%, structural damage risk from overpressure decreases even though radical activity remains high, because oxygen deficiency limits complete combustion.

D. The fact that peak overpressure occurs at lower methane concentrations than peak radical concentration suggests that mixture stoichiometry and energy release rate, not radical peak density, primarily determine the macroscopic pressure hazard.

E. Suppressing radical activity at 8–9% methane would reduce post-explosion CO formation because incomplete combustion at that concentration range is driven by radical chain propagation rather than oxygen availability.
F. A safety intervention that reduces methane concentration below 4% is more likely to reduce overpressure hazard than one targeting radical suppression at 8–9% methane.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states temporal decoupling between radical activity and macroscopic overpressure; radical peak at 8–9% does not coincide with pressure peak at 4–6%.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly contradicts direct causation of peak overpressure by peak radical concentration; this is the primary causal overclaim the evidence refutes.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Above 8% methane, oxygen deficiency causes incomplete combustion; overpressure peak is at 4–6%, so above 8% the pressure hazard is reduced despite high radical activity.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Overpressure peaks at 4–6% (near stoichiometric) while radicals peak at 8–9%; this decoupling supports stoichiometry and energy release as primary overpressure drivers.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence attributes CO increase above 8% methane to oxygen deficiency, not radical chain propagation; this option misidentifies the governing mechanism for incomplete combustion.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Keeping methane below 4% (below the LEL range) prevents reaching the peak overpressure regime at 4–6%; this directly addresses the overpressure hazard rather than a decoupled radical metric.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Temporal decoupling between radical peak concentration and peak overpressure, and its implication for suppression strategy design
analysis.primary_boundary: Radical concentration peak does not coincide with overpressure peak; suppression targeting radicals at 8–9% does not address the primary pressure hazard at 4–6%
intended_error_modes: causal overclaim linking radical density to overpressure
mechanism substitution attributing CO formation to radicals rather than oxygen deficiency
disallowed_extrapolations: radical suppression effectiveness on overpressure without evidence of coupling
CO formation mechanism from radical activity at high methane

solver_view.stem: A battery energy storage system using LFP cells operates across a range of SOC levels. During a fire investigation, analysts find that the vented gas mixture ignited but the explosion was less severe than predicted by models calibrated on low-SOC data. The cells were confirmed at approximately 90% SOC at the time of thermal runaway. Which of the following statements about the explosion hazard characterization at this SOC are consistent with the evidence?
options: A. At approximately 90% SOC, the gas mixture contains a substantially higher proportion of H2 than at 25% SOC, which expands the range of mixture concentrations capable of sustaining ignition.
B. The lower explosion limit at 90% SOC is expected to be dramatically lower than at 25% SOC, explaining why ignition occurred at a lower gas concentration than the low-SOC model predicted.

C. The wider explosive window at high SOC primarily reflects an expansion of the upper explosion limit rather than a shift in the lower explosion limit, meaning the ignition threshold is not substantially reduced.

D. If the actual gas-air mixture concentration at ignition was above the upper explosion limit for the high-SOC gas, the mixture would not have supported propagating combustion regardless of the H2 content.

E. The lower severity than predicted could be consistent with the actual mixture concentration being outside the explosive window, even though the SOC was high and the gas composition was more combustible.
F. Because H2 proportion increases with SOC, the peak overpressure during any ignition event necessarily increases proportionally with SOC.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: H2 surges to 73.3% at 100% SOC from 42.9% at 25% SOC; UEL expands substantially, widening the concentration range supporting ignition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: LEL remains relatively stable (5–11%) across all SOC levels; a dramatic LEL reduction at high SOC is not supported and contradicts the evidence.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states UEL expands from ~17% to ~55% with SOC while LEL remains 5–11%; the widening is UEL-driven, not LEL-driven—a counterintuitive but evidence-supported conclusion.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Above the UEL, mixture is too rich to support propagating combustion regardless of H2 content; this is a fundamental combustion principle consistent with the defined explosive window.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Lower severity is admissibly explained by mixture concentration falling outside the explosive window even at high SOC; high combustibility of composition does not guarantee the mixture was within explosive limits at the point of ignition.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Peak overpressure depends on methane concentration and mixture stoichiometry, not solely on H2 proportion; proportional scaling of overpressure with SOC is not supported by evidence.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: SOC-dependent explosive window behavior and admissible explanations for lower-than-predicted explosion severity at high SOC
analysis.primary_boundary: UEL expansion dominates the SOC effect on explosive window; LEL is relatively stable; high SOC increases post-ignition hazard but does not lower ignition threshold substantially
intended_error_modes: attributing LEL reduction to high SOC
assuming proportional overpressure scaling with H2 content or SOC
disallowed_extrapolations: LEL shift at high SOC
direct proportionality between SOC and peak overpressure

solver_view.prompt: An LFP battery energy storage installation vents thermal runaway gases into a shared space with a natural gas supply line that has a slow leak. A safety engineer notes that at high SOC, the vented gas has a much higher proportion of H2 and a much lower proportion of CO2 compared to low SOC. The engineer also knows that explosion overpressure peaks at a specific methane concentration range, that above a higher methane threshold incomplete combustion produces elevated CO, and that free radical concentration peaks at a different methane concentration than peak overpressure. Explain why designing a suppression system based solely on minimizing free radical activity at the methane concentration producing peak radical density would be inadequate, and identify what additional hazard—distinct from overpressure—becomes dominant above the threshold where oxygen deficiency occurs. In your answer, address how the SOC-dependent shift in gas composition affects the adequacy of a fixed CO2-based suppression strategy.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Targeting radical suppression at the methane concentration producing peak radical density (approximately 8–9%) is inadequate for overpressure control because peak explosion overpressure occurs at a lower methane concentration (approximately 4–6%), revealing a temporal and mechanistic decoupling between radical activity and macroscopic pressure output. Radical peak density does not coincide with the most structurally damaging explosion condition, so a suppression strategy optimized for radical reduction at 8–9% methane would not address the primary overpressure hazard. Above approximately 8% methane, oxygen deficiency causes incomplete combustion, shifting the dominant hazard from overpressure to toxicity: CO production increases sharply while CO2 decreases, creating a secondary toxic and asphyxiation hazard for occupants and responders. Regarding SOC dependence: at high SOC, CO2 proportion in the vented gas drops from approximately 50% to approximately 16%. Since CO2 is the primary fire-retardant component in the vented mixture, a fixed CO2-based suppression system calibrated for low-SOC conditions will be substantially undersized at high SOC, where the gas is simultaneously more combustible (higher H2) and provides less intrinsic fire-retardant dilution. The suppression strategy must therefore account for both the methane concentration regime governing the dominant hazard mode and the SOC-dependent reduction in the vented gas's self-suppressing capacity.
must_include: peak overpressure occurs at 4–6% methane, not at the radical peak concentration of 8–9%
temporal or mechanistic decoupling between radical activity and macroscopic overpressure
above approximately 8% methane, oxygen deficiency causes incomplete combustion with elevated CO and reduced CO2
the dominant hazard above 8% methane shifts to toxicity rather than overpressure
CO2 proportion in vented gas decreases substantially from low to high SOC
CO2-based suppression effective at low SOC is insufficient at high SOC due to reduced CO2 fraction in vented gas
disallowed_extrapolations: claiming radical suppression has no role in any explosion mitigation strategy
asserting that high SOC guarantees ignition or detonation
stating that LEL decreases substantially with SOC
claiming peak overpressure scales proportionally with H2 content or SOC

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Integration of radical-overpressure decoupling, oxygen-deficiency toxicity regime, and SOC-dependent suppression adequacy into a multi-hazard safety assessment
analysis.primary_boundary: Radical peak concentration does not govern overpressure peak; above 8% methane the hazard mode shifts; CO2 suppression capacity degrades at high SOC
intended_error_modes: conflating radical peak with overpressure peak
ignoring the toxicity hazard shift above 8% methane
assuming CO2-based suppression remains adequate regardless of SOC
